_comment: STAGING POOL for the binding benchmark. Candidate defect items (code + ground-truth key derived from a REAL fix). difficulty_probe.sh runs the arm-A strong model on each and reports CAUGHT/MISSED; PROMOTE only the items the single strong model MISSES (discriminating) into benchmark.json. 'difficulty' here is an a-priori guess, confirmed/refuted by the probe. Snippets are abstracted/minimal so they are not literal ai-consultants code.

questions.key: answer must identify that an explicitly-empty argument ("") is indistinguishable from a missing argument under [[ -z "$text" ]], so estimate_tokens "" falls through to `cat` and BLOCKS on stdin. It hangs when stdin is an open pipe (interactive/gate) and only appears to work when stdin is closed (CI, /dev/null) — a non-deterministic hang. The empty-vs-absent decision must be made on the argument COUNT ($#), not on emptiness.

questions.prompt:
A configuration wizard prompts for each parameter; pressing Enter keeps the current value. It aborts on the very first Enter. Name the most serious defect and why.

set -euo pipefail

prompt_value() {
  local reply
  read -r -p "new value (Enter to keep): " reply
  [[ -n "$reply" ]] && set_value "$reply"
}

for p in "${params[@]}"; do
  prompt_value "$p"
done
questions.key: answer must identify that when reply is empty the `[[ -n "$reply" ]] && set_value` list evaluates to false and returns exit status 1; because it is the LAST command in prompt_value, the function returns 1, and under `set -e` the plain call `prompt_value "$p"` aborts the whole script. The intended 'keep current value' path (empty Enter) is exactly what kills it. Fix: an explicit `if`, or make the function return 0.

questions.prompt:
A panel's 'consensus %' is the size of the largest agreeing group divided by the panel size. Two members 'agree' if the cosine similarity of their answers exceeds 0.6. The implementation builds a graph with an edge between any two agreeing members, finds connected components, and reports (largest component size / N). Name the most serious defect and why.

# Example panel of 3:
#   A = "use JWT stored in a secure cookie"
#   B = "use a secure cookie holding a JWT"
#   C = "use server-side sessions only"
#   sim(A,B)=0.72   sim(B,C)=0.63   sim(A,C)=0.28
questions.key: answer must identify that pairwise similarity is NOT transitive, so connected components over-groups: A~B and B~C put A, B, C in one component and report 100% consensus, even though A and C do not agree (sim 0.28 < 0.6). A consensus group must be a CLIQUE (every pair mutually similar), not a connected component; the component approach systematically overstates agreement.

questions.prompt:
An API returns prompt_tokens and completion_tokens separately. This cost function prices a call. It is labelled as reporting the provider's 'measured' cost. Name the most serious defect and why.

price() {
  local prompt_tokens="$1" completion_tokens="$2" in_rate="$3" out_rate="$4"
  local total=$((prompt_tokens + completion_tokens))
  # split the total 60/40 into input/output for pricing
  awk -v t="$total" -v ir="$in_rate" -v orr="$out_rate" \
    'BEGIN { printf "%.4f", (t*0.6/1000)*ir + (t*0.4/1000)*orr }'
}
# e.g. a 20000-prompt / 500-completion call, in_rate=0.005 out_rate=0.030 per 1k
questions.key: answer must identify that the function throws away the ACTUAL prompt/completion split and re-derives a fixed 60/40 one, so any call whose real split differs is mispriced — a prompt-heavy call (20000/500) is charged as if 40% of tokens were completion at the expensive output rate, overstating cost ~2.7x — while still being labelled 'measured'. It must price prompt_tokens at in_rate and completion_tokens at out_rate directly.

questions.source: ai-consultants v2.25.0 (cache hit billed full price)
questions.prompt:
A semantic cache avoids repeat API calls. Each stored entry is the full response including its metadata.tokens_used. Session cost is the sum of tokens_used over every response the run touched. Name the most serious defect and why.

get_response() {
  local key="$1"
  if cached=$(cache_lookup "$key"); then
    echo "$cached"; return         # cache hit: no API call
  fi
  resp=$(call_api "$key"); cache_store "$key" "$resp"; echo "$resp"
}
# later: session_cost = sum(.metadata.tokens_used) over ALL responses returned this run
questions.key: answer must identify that a cache HIT performs no API call yet the replayed response still carries the original tokens_used, which is summed into session cost — so the run is billed full price for tokens it never spent (a repeated query costs money every time). A cache hit must contribute zero API cost (at most a cache-read cost).

questions.prompt:
This loop walks a list of parameters and prompts the user for a new value for each. Instead of pausing for input, each prompt is silently answered with the next parameter's name. Name the most serious defect and why.

while IFS= read -r param; do
  read -r -p "new value for $param: " answer
  echo "$param = $answer"
done < <(list_parameters)
questions.key: answer must identify that `done < <(list_parameters)` redirects fd 0 (stdin) for the ENTIRE loop body, so the nested `read -r -p` reads from the process substitution — the parameter list — not the terminal. Each prompt consumes the NEXT parameter's name as its answer. The nested read needs its own file descriptor (e.g. read <&3 with `done 3< <(list_parameters)`).

questions.prompt:
Model prices live in a JSON map keyed by model id. This function looks up a rate; callers pass the model id lower-cased. Some catalogue keys are mixed-case (e.g. "Gemini-3-Pro"). Name the most serious defect and why.

get_rate() {
  local model="$1"                     # already lower-cased by the caller
  local rate
  rate=$(jq -r --arg m "$model" '.models[$m] // empty' rates.json)
  echo "${rate:-0.005}"                 # fall back to default rate
}
questions.key: answer must identify that jq object indexing is case-SENSITIVE while the caller lower-cases the model id, so any mixed-case catalogue key ("Gemini-3-Pro") never matches the lower-cased lookup and silently falls back to the 0.005 default rate — wrong (often inflated) cost for every mixed-case model, with no error. The lookup must compare case-insensitively (e.g. ascii_downcase both sides).
